file_name: Crawford et al. - 2023 - Using artificial intelligence as a melanoma screening tool in self-referred patients.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used the FotoFinder Moleanalyzer Pro® (Version 6.0), a proprietary computer software utilizing deep learning and convolutional neural networks, to analyze macroscopic and dermoscopic images of skin lesions for the detection and screening of melanoma and other skin cancers in self-referred patients.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Article' and describes a single-centre observational study involving patient recruitment, lesion imaging with the FotoFinder Moleanalyzer Pro®, and pathological validation. It reports original results including sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy metrics for AI versus dermatologists, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Using Artificial Intelligence as a Melanoma Screening Tool in Self-Referred Patients
journal: Journal of Cutaneous Medicine and Surgery
brief_summary: This single-centre observational study evaluated the feasibility of using the FotoFinder Moleanalyzer Pro® AI system to screen for melanoma in self-referred patients concerned about pigmented lesions. Conducted in Halifax, Nova Scotia, the study recruited 318 participants who underwent macroscopic and dermoscopic imaging analyzed by the AI and blinded dermatologists. Suspicious lesions identified by either the AI or clinicians were excised and validated by pathology, revealing 17 malignancies including 10 melanomas. The AI demonstrated a sensitivity of 64.7% and specificity of 75.76%, with a diagnostic accuracy of 73.56% that was non-inferior to experienced dermatologists. Although the AI missed six diagnostically challenging in situ melanomas, the authors conclude it serves as a practical screening aid to provide timely access to diagnosis for high-risk individuals.

lesion_types_studied.details: 10 confirmed melanomas were found among 17 malignancies. The study focused on screening for melanoma in self-referred patients. Includes melanoma in situ and invasive melanomas (e.g., Breslow depth 0.2 mm, 0.5 mm, 1.0 mm).
6 pigmented basal cell carcinomas were confirmed among the 17 malignancies. Included in the definition of malignancy for statistical evaluation.

paper_terms: pigmented squamous cell carcinoma in situ
lesion_types_studied.details: 1 pigmented squamous cell carcinoma in situ was confirmed among the 17 malignancies. Included in the definition of malignancy for statistical evaluation.
Most common benign diagnosis (49.1% of excluded lesions clinically, also present in excised lesions). Trained medical students were trained to recognize this to exclude obvious cases.

all_task_types.target: Malignancy (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, or squamous cell carcinoma) vs. benign lesion
all_task_types.details: The AI system (FotoFinder Moleanalyzer Pro®) was used to flag possible melanomas and malignancies. It produced a binary output indicating whether a lesion was suspicious for malignancy (flagged for excision) or not, using a probability threshold of 0.8 to indicate possible malignancy. The study evaluated sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy for diagnosing malignancy.

datasets_used.name: Self-referred patient cohort (Halifax, Nova Scotia)
datasets_used.description: A single-centre observational cohort of self-referred patients concerned about pigmented skin lesions, recruited from advertisements in 2 hospitals and 4 family practice clinics in Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada. Participants underwent macroscopic and dermoscopic imaging using the FotoFinder Medicam 800HD and analysis by the FotoFinder Moleanalyzer Pro® AI system, followed by pathological validation of excised lesions.

use.testing: The cohort served as the test set for evaluating the diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, AUC) of the pre-existing FotoFinder Moleanalyzer Pro® AI system and blinded dermatologists against pathological ground truth.

datasets_used.name: FotoFinder Moleanalyzer Pro® Version 6.0 (Internal Training Data)
datasets_used.description: The proprietary deep learning model used for analysis. The paper states the system has been shown to identify melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and most squamous cell carcinomas in previous studies, but does not specify the internal dataset used to train this specific version of the software.

use.training: The AI system was a pre-trained proprietary tool; specific details of its training dataset are not reported in this paper.
use.other: Pre-trained model applied to the study cohort.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the AI system and dermatologists in identifying malignancy (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, or squamous cell carcinoma) versus benign lesions.

statistical_methods_used.method: Positive and Negative Likelihood Ratios (PLR, NLR)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the utility of the AI and dermatologist diagnoses in ruling in or ruling out malignancy.

statistical_methods_used.method: Positive and Negative Predictive Values (PPV, NPV)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine the probability that a lesion is malignant given a positive or negative diagnosis by the AI or dermatologists.
To determine the overall probability that the lesion was correctly classified, defined as sensitivity × prevalence + specificity × (1 − prevalence).
To calculate the efficiency of the screening method by dividing the total number of biopsies by the number of skin cancers found.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operator Characteristic (ROC) curves and Area Under the Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the overall diagnostic accuracy of the AI and dermatologists in recommending excision versus pathological results; AUC (or C-statistic) was used to summarize overall accuracy.
To assess inter-rater variability (agreement) among the dermatologists regarding the decision to excise or not.
To provide uncertainty estimates for the Fleiss’ kappa value and the AUC values.
To determine the statistical significance of the AUC values for each reviewer.

hypothesis_testing.test: Asymptotic significance testing for the Area Under the Curve (AUC) of Receiver Operator Characteristic (ROC) curves
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: The Area Under the Curve (AUC) for the reviewer's diagnostic accuracy is equal to 0.5 (no discriminative ability, equivalent to random chance).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The Area Under the Curve (AUC) for the reviewer's diagnostic accuracy is significantly different from 0.5 (indicating discriminative ability greater than random chance).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study reported asymptotic significance values (p-values) for the AUC of each reviewer. The AI system (p=0.010) and the external dermatologist (p=0.003) showed statistically significant diagnostic accuracy (AUC significantly greater than 0.5) at the 0.05 alpha level. The internal dermatologists (Derm 1, Derm 2, Derm 3, Derm 4) and the resident did not show statistically significant AUCs (p > 0.05), although the text notes that scores between 0.7 and 0.8 are generally considered acceptable diagnostic accuracy.

Self-referred patient cohort (Halifax, N.notes: The study evaluated 381 lesions from 318 participants. 17 malignancies were confirmed (10 melanomas, 6 BCC, 1 SCC in situ). The AI flagged 27 lesions for excision, identifying 11 cancers. The external dermatologist had the highest sensitivity (70.6%) and AUC (0.732). The AI's diagnostic accuracy was non-inferior to experienced dermatologists. Inter-rater reliability among dermatologists was fair (Fleiss’ kappa 0.22, 95% CI: 0.16-0.28). The AI missed 6 in situ melanomas which were diagnostically challenging for both AI and dermatopathologists.

architecture_used.pretraining: Proprietary deep learning model; specific training dataset details not reported.
architecture_used.input_size: Macroscopic and dermoscopic images (specific dimensions not reported).

architecture_details.description: Proprietary computer software utilizing deep learning and convolutional neural networks for analyzing macroscopic and dermoscopic images.

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Binary classification (suspicious for malignancy vs. not)
classification_or_output_head.threshold: Probability threshold of 0.8 used to indicate possible malignancy.

preprocessing_techniques: Image acquisition using FotoFinder Medicam 800HD for macroscopic and dermoscopic imaging

Technical exclusions applied to images: lesions inaccessible to camera, lesions in hairy areas, lesions on lips or genitalia, lesions on face, lesions close to tattoos, lesions close to other skin disorders, lesions in Fitzpatrick skin types IV-VI, previously excised lesions

Clinical exclusions applied to images: obvious seborrheic keratosis, cherry angioma, actinic keratosis, ecchymosis, obvious dermatofibroma, porokeratosis, other common skin lesions including non-melanoma skin cancer
conclusions: The AI system (FotoFinder Moleanalyzer Pro®) demonstrated a sensitivity of 64.7% and specificity of 75.76% for detecting malignancy, with a diagnostic accuracy of 73.56%.

The AI's diagnostic accuracy was non-inferior to experienced dermatologists, with performance comparable to the external dermatologist (accuracy 74.72%, AUC 0.732) and the AI (accuracy 73.56%, AUC 0.702).
The AI achieved the lowest Number Needed to Biopsy (NNB) of 2.25, indicating high efficiency in identifying skin cancers compared to dermatologists.

The AI missed six diagnostically challenging in situ melanomas that showed ambiguous atypical melanocytic proliferations, which were also difficult for dermatologists and dermatopathologists to diagnose.
The study acknowledges technical limitations of the AI, including inability to image lesions in hairy areas, on the face, lips, or genitalia, and in patients with Fitzpatrick skin types IV-VI.

The authors conclude that AI can serve as a practical skin cancer screening aid for self-referred patients concerned about pigmented lesions, providing timely access to diagnosis despite its technical and diagnostic limitations.
important_remarks: Single-centre observational study with a population predominantly of Fitzpatrick skin types I-III (mainly of Scottish and Irish origin), limiting generalizability to darker skin types (IV-VI) which were technically excluded.
The AI system (FotoFinder Moleanalyzer Pro®) had significant technical limitations, unable to image lesions on the face, hairy areas, lips, genitalia, or close to tattoos/other disorders.

The AI missed 6 cases of melanoma in situ; however, these were identified as diagnostically challenging 'atypical melanocytic proliferations' by dermatopathologists, suggesting the difficulty was inherent to the lesions rather than solely an AI failure.
The study cohort was self-referred and high-risk, with 28.9% having a family history of melanoma, which may inflate the prevalence of malignancy compared to general screening populations.
No external validation was performed; the AI was evaluated against a small sample of dermatologists (3 experienced, 1 resident, 1 external) in a single clinical setting.

The AI's performance (AUC 0.702) was non-inferior to the external dermatologist (AUC 0.732) and internal dermatologists, but several internal dermatologists did not achieve statistically significant AUCs (p > 0.05) in this specific cohort.
Inter-rater reliability among dermatologists was only 'fair' (Fleiss’ kappa 0.22), indicating significant variability in clinical decision-making regarding excision.
The study did not report the internal training dataset details for the proprietary AI model, preventing assessment of potential data leakage or training set overlap with the test cohort.
The Number Needed to Biopsy (NNB) metric, while useful for efficiency, does not account for false negatives, potentially overestimating the clinical utility of the AI in a screening context.
